text:
Profile: Maya, age 23, just started her first job earning $65,000. She has $8,000 in student loans at 5.5% interest and no savings. Goal: Build wealth for long-term financial independence.

Recommended approach: (1) Build a $3,000 starter emergency fund before aggressive debt payoff. (2) Contribute enough to her 401(k) to get the full employer match (3%). (3) Pay down student loans while simultaneously contributing to a Roth IRA ($200/month to start). (4) Once loans are paid off, redirect loan payment to investments. Time is Maya's greatest asset — $300/month invested at 8% from age 23 grows to over $1 million by age 65. Investment allocation: 90% equities (60% U.S. total market, 30% international, 10% small-cap value), 10% bonds. Gradually increase bond allocation as she approaches retirement.

title: Case Study: Couple in Their 30s Buying First Home
text:
Profile: James and Lisa, both 34, combined income $160,000. They are saving for a down payment on a $500,000 home and want to put down 20% ($100,000) within 3 years.

Recommended approach: Down payment savings should NOT be in stocks given the 3-year timeline. Optimal vehicles: high-yield savings account, 2-year CDs, and short-term Treasury bills. Currently saving $2,500/month, they can reach the goal in approximately 3.5 years assuming 4.5% yield. In parallel: continue maxing employer 401(k) match, fund Roth IRAs ($7,000 each). First-time homebuyer programs may allow lower down payment (3–5%) if they qualify. Important: Don't sacrifice retirement savings entirely for home savings — the compounding loss of not investing in your 30s is substantial.

title: Case Study: High-Income Professional Maximizing Tax Efficiency
text:
Profile: Dr. Sarah Chen, 42, physician earning $400,000/year. She is in the 35% federal tax bracket, maxes out her 401(k), and is looking to optimize further.

Tax-efficiency priority stack: (1) Backdoor Roth IRA contribution ($7,000) — income is too high for direct Roth contribution. (2) HSA contribution ($4,150) if enrolled in HDHP. (3) After-tax 401(k) with Roth conversion (Mega Backdoor Roth) — up to $66,000 total 401(k) contribution in 2024. (4) 457(b) plan if available through her hospital employer (additional $23,000 deferral). (5) Municipal bond funds in taxable account — tax-equivalent yield is excellent at her tax rate. (6) Tax-loss harvesting in taxable account. Consider SEP-IRA or defined benefit plan if she has any self-employment income through a private practice.

title: Case Study: Retiree Seeking Sustainable Income
text:
Profile: Robert, age 68, recently retired. Portfolio: $1.2M (60% stocks, 40% bonds). Social Security: $2,200/month. Monthly expenses: $5,500. Gap to fill from portfolio: $3,300/month ($39,600/year).

Analysis: $39,600 / $1,200,000 = 3.3% withdrawal rate — comfortably within sustainable range. Recommended structure: (1) 2 years of cash in HYSA for living expenses ($80,000). (2) Bond ladder for years 3–7 using individual Treasury bonds and CDs. (3) Diversified equity portfolio (dividend-focused) for long-term growth. At-risk assets: review for any excessive concentrations. RMD planning: at $1.2M, RMDs will begin at a manageable level but will grow — Roth conversions in lower-income years (ages 60–72) could have reduced this obligation. Annual tax planning: harvest losses in equities to offset bond interest.

title: Case Study: Business Owner Selling a Company
text:
Profile: Michael, 55, sold his manufacturing business for $3.5M (cash deal). Long-term capital gains tax of ~$500,000 (20% federal + 3.8% NIIT + state) will be owed. Net proceeds: approximately $3M.

Recommended approach: (1) Qualified Opportunity Zone (QOZ) investment to defer capital gains — investing gains in a QOZ fund within 180 days defers tax to 2026 and reduces it if held. (2) Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) — contribute appreciated assets, receive income stream, defer capital gains, charitable deduction. (3) Immediate: deploy $1.5M into diversified portfolio while $1.5M is invested in QOZ or used for next business venture. (4) Maximize all retirement account contributions for this last high-income year. (5) Revise estate plan — new estate size requires review of estate tax strategy.

title: Case Study: Divorce and Financial Restart at 48
text:
Profile: Patricia, 48, recently divorced. Received $350,000 in investment accounts, keeps the family home (value: $425,000, mortgage: $180,000), and will receive $1,800/month in support payments for 5 years. Income: $72,000/year (new job). Goals: rebuild retirement savings, maintain home, achieve financial independence.

Priority plan: (1) Review and update all beneficiary designations immediately — remove ex-spouse. (2) Update will and powers of attorney. (3) Evaluate whether to keep home — carrying costs (mortgage, property tax, insurance, maintenance) should not exceed 30% of gross income. (4) Build 6-month emergency fund ($25,000). (5) Maximize 401(k) contributions — at 48, she still has 15+ working years. (6) Review investment accounts received: re-register QDRO (qualified domestic relations order) if any came from ex's 401(k). Investment allocation: 70% equities/30% bonds given age and career restart.

title: Case Study: Inheritance Planning for a 35-Year-Old
text:
Profile: David, 35, unexpectedly inherited $180,000 from a grandparent. He has $22,000 in credit card debt (18% interest), $45,000 in student loans (6%), a healthy 401(k), and no taxable brokerage account.

Priority allocation: (1) Immediately pay off all credit card debt ($22,000) — guaranteed 18% return. (2) Build emergency fund to 6 months ($18,000 in HYSA). (3) Pay off student loans ($45,000) — 6% guaranteed return vs. uncertain investment returns. (4) Remaining $95,000: open taxable brokerage and invest in low-cost index funds (VTSAX + VXUS + BND). (5) Simultaneously max Roth IRA for this year ($7,000). Important: Windfall discipline — avoid lifestyle inflation. The $95,000 in index funds at 7% real return becomes $1.35M at age 65. This inheritance can transform his retirement trajectory.

title: Case Study: Pre-Retiree at 58 — 7 Years to Retirement
text:
Profile: Carol, 58, planning to retire at 65. Portfolio: $620,000 (70% equities/30% bonds). Income: $95,000/year. Social Security projected: $2,400/month at 65 ($3,400/month if delayed to 70). Monthly expenses in retirement: estimated $6,000.

7-year countdown plan: (1) Maximize 401(k) catch-up contributions ($30,500/year) — over 7 years adds approximately $215,000+ in contributions plus growth. (2) Consider delaying Social Security to 70 — $1,000/month more = $12,000/year more, break-even at approximately age 82. (3) Begin building cash and bond ladder for first 5 years of retirement income. (4) Gradually de-risk: reduce to 60/40 at 60, 55/45 at 62, 50/50 at 65. (5) Project Medicare costs at 65 and plan for healthcare bridge. (6) Model long-term care insurance — late 50s is optimal purchase age.

title: Case Study: Young Family — Balancing College and Retirement Savings
text:
Profile: Tom and Amy, 38 and 36. Combined income: $145,000. Two children ages 4 and 7. Current retirement savings: $180,000. No college savings yet.

Prioritization framework: Always prioritize retirement over college. You can borrow for college; you cannot borrow for retirement. Recommended allocation of monthly surplus ($1,800/month after expenses and 401(k) match capture): (1) $600/month to 529 plans ($400 for 7-year-old, $200 for 4-year-old) — should accumulate $100,000–$120,000 by college age assuming 6% return. (2) $1,200/month to Roth IRAs ($600 each). College cost estimate: 4-year public university currently $130,000–$140,000 total; 4-year private: $280,000–$320,000. Gap can be filled with financial aid, work-study, and student loans. Retirement gap cannot.

title: Case Study: Widow at 71 Managing Inherited IRA
text:
Profile: Eleanor, 71, recently widowed. Inherited husband's $450,000 IRA. Her own IRA: $280,000. Social Security survivor benefit: $2,800/month. Pension: $1,200/month. Monthly expenses: $4,500.

Key decisions: (1) As a spousal beneficiary, Eleanor can roll the inherited IRA into her own IRA — she should do this if she doesn't need the funds immediately, as it delays RMDs. (2) RMD calculation on combined $730,000 IRA at age 71: approximately $24,700/year — well within her income needs given pension + Social Security covers expenses. (3) Investment allocation: At 71, with income covered, she can maintain 50% equity for growth to pass to heirs. Consider a total return approach rather than income-focused. (4) Estate planning: update beneficiary designations, consider Roth conversions to reduce future RMDs and leave tax-free assets to children.

title: Case Study: Early Retirement Seeker (FIRE) at 42
text:
Profile: Alex, 42, seeks to retire at 50. Current portfolio: $1.1M. Annual expenses: $60,000. Annual savings rate: $80,000. Goal: $1.5M portfolio to support $60,000/year withdrawals (4% rule).

Analysis: At current savings and assumed 7% real return, Alex reaches $1.5M in approximately 4 years (age 46) — ahead of schedule. Key challenges for early retirement: (1) Healthcare: Must fund healthcare from age 50–65 until Medicare eligibility — budget $15,000–$20,000/year (ACA marketplace). (2) Roth conversion ladder: IRA funds are accessible at 59½ without penalty, but with 9-year bridge needed. Build taxable account and Roth ladder (convert now, access principal in 5 years). (3) Sequence of returns risk is heightened with a 50+ year retirement horizon — consider 3.0–3.5% withdrawal rate. (4) Plan for meaningful activity and social connection — non-financial aspects of retirement are critical.

title: Case Study: Recent College Graduate With Significant Student Debt
text:
Profile: Jordan, 24, earned a law degree with $185,000 in federal student loans at average 6.5% interest. Starting salary at a law firm: $90,000. Choosing between aggressive debt payoff and investing.

Recommended framework: (1) Explore income-driven repayment (IDR) plans and Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) if pursuing public interest law — forgiveness after 10 years of qualifying payments. (2) If private sector: capture full 401(k) match first ($2,700 match on $9,000 contribution at 3% match). (3) Build $5,000 emergency fund. (4) Contribute $200/month to Roth IRA while on lower salary (before income rises). (5) Aggressively pay down highest-rate loans first. At $90K income, allocate 25–30% of take-home ($1,500–$1,800/month) to debt service above minimum payments. At this rate, $185,000 can be paid in 8–10 years.

title: Case Study: Tech Employee with Stock Compensation
text:
Profile: Kevin, 35, software engineer at a tech company. Salary: $200,000 plus $150,000 in RSUs vesting annually. Current net worth: $800,000, but $500,000 concentrated in employer stock. Concern: Over-concentration risk.

Diversification strategy: (1) As RSUs vest, sell immediately (or soon after) to diversify — the company has already compensated you with the shares. Holding creates concentration risk. (2) If single stock already exceeds 10–15% of net worth, gradually sell and diversify even if capital gains are owed. (3) If employer stock is in 401(k), Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) rules may offer a tax-efficient distribution strategy. (4) Build diversified portfolio with RSU proceeds: 60% VTI + 20% VXUS + 10% BND + 10% AVUV (small-cap value tilt). (5) Beware the correlation of concentrated stock risk with employment risk — if company struggles, you may lose job and see stock decline simultaneously.

title: Case Study: Immigrant First-Generation Wealth Builder
text:
Profile: Priya, 32, immigrated to the U.S. at age 22. Currently earning $78,000, no employer-sponsored retirement plan available (small non-profit employer). Limited knowledge of U.S. financial system. Goals: build emergency fund, understand accounts, invest for future.

Step-by-step plan: (1) Open HYSA at an online bank — earn 4–5% on emergency savings goal of $15,000. (2) Open Traditional IRA or Roth IRA at Fidelity/Vanguard/Schwab — fully deductible IRA at $78K income without employer plan. Start with $100/month, increase as budget allows. (3) No 401(k) available — self-employed individuals can open a Solo 401(k) or SEP-IRA if any freelance income exists. (4) Invest IRA in a single target date fund for simplicity (e.g., Vanguard Target Retirement 2055). (5) Establish ITIN or SSN-based credit history if not already built — credit score impacts borrowing costs for future home purchase.

title: Case Study: Small Business Owner Planning Retirement
text:
Profile: Frank, 52, owner of a profitable plumbing business generating $250,000/year in net income. No retirement plan established. Wants to retire in 10 years.

Business owner retirement plan options, ranked by maximum contribution: (1) Defined Benefit (DB) Plan: Can shelter $150,000–$200,000+/year at age 52 — dramatically reduces current taxes and builds substantial retirement asset. Best option if maximizing tax deferral is the priority. Actuarial calculations required. (2) Solo 401(k): $66,000 in 2024 ($73,500 with catch-up at 50+) — employee contribution + employer profit sharing up to 25% of compensation. Simpler administration. (3) SEP-IRA: 25% of net self-employment income, up to $66,000. Simplest option. Also consider: whether to structure business sale for retirement funding; succession planning (key man insurance, buy-sell agreement).

title: Case Study: Sudden Job Loss at 55
text:
Profile: Linda, 55, laid off from a $120,000/year corporate job after 20 years. Severance: 4 months ($40,000). 401(k): $380,000. Cash: $35,000. Mortgage: $1,200/month. No other debt.

Immediate priorities: (1) Apply for unemployment insurance immediately — up to 26 weeks in most states. (2) Activate COBRA health insurance — expensive but provides continuity; ACA marketplace may be cheaper given reduced income. (3) Create a reduced-spending budget for job search period. (4) Do NOT raid the 401(k) — withdrawals before 59½ incur 10% penalty plus income tax. Exception: 55 rule — separating from service at 55+ allows penalty-free 401(k) withdrawals from that specific employer's plan. (5) Job search: Update LinkedIn, networking is most effective for senior roles. (6) If job market is difficult, evaluate whether skills can be monetized through consulting while searching. Budget allows 18+ months of coverage before needing to tap investments.

title: Case Study: Elderly Parent Long-Term Care Planning
text:
Profile: Helen, 78, and her daughter Susan, 52. Helen has moderate dementia and can no longer live alone. Helen's assets: $280,000 in savings/investments, $1,400/month Social Security, home ($320,000 value, no mortgage). Monthly memory care cost in their state: $7,500–$8,500.

Planning analysis: At $8,000/month, Helen's $280,000 savings last approximately 35 months without investment growth. Options: (1) Sell home to fund care — proceeds would fund 5+ additional years. (2) Medicaid planning: Medicaid covers long-term care for those who have 'spent down' assets to state-defined limits. Requires 5-year look-back on asset transfers — consult an elder law attorney. (3) PACE program (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) may provide care coordination for those who qualify. Lesson for Susan: Purchase long-term care insurance now in her 50s — premium is $2,500–$4,000/year versus $96,000/year for care.

title: Case Study: Retiring Teacher with Pension
text:
Profile: Maria, 60, public school teacher with 30 years of service. Defined benefit pension: $4,200/month at age 62 (80% of final 3-year average salary). 403(b) balance: $95,000. No Social Security (public sector job). Home equity: $180,000. Monthly expenses: $4,800.

Analysis: Maria's pension nearly covers her expenses — a favorable position. Key considerations: (1) Pension election: Evaluate single-life vs. joint-and-survivor option if married — survivor benefit reduces monthly amount but provides protection for spouse. (2) No Social Security: Public sector workers may be affected by the Windfall Elimination Provision (WEP) if they have some Social Security earnings. Consult SSA for accurate benefit projection. (3) 403(b): Serves as supplemental income and emergency reserve — invest conservatively (60/40) since pension provides income floor. (4) Retirement healthcare bridge: does school district provide retiree health insurance? If not, budget for ACA marketplace until Medicare at 65.

title: Case Study: Military Veteran Transitioning to Civilian Financial Planning
text:
Profile: Marcus, 38, recently separated from military service after 15 years. Will receive Reserve retirement pay at age 60 (approximately $1,800/month). VA disability rating: 40% ($700/month, tax-free). Starting civilian career at $85,000/year. TSP (Thrift Savings Plan) balance: $145,000.

Financial plan: (1) Roll TSP to IRA or leave in TSP — TSP has excellent low-cost funds (G Fund for short-term, C/S/I funds for equity). TSP's institutional pricing (0.04% expense ratio) is competitive. Consider keeping TSP while adding new employer 401(k). (2) VA disability: Tax-free income — use for accelerated savings. (3) GI Bill education benefits: If planning graduate school, maximize remaining GI Bill benefits. (4) VA home loan: No down payment required — significant benefit for first home purchase. (5) Vesting timeline: Civilian employer 401(k) match vesting schedule — plan to stay through vesting. Marcus has strong foundations and meaningful guaranteed income at 60.

title: Case Study: Concentrated Position in Single Stock — Managing Risk
text:
Profile: Barbara, 61, long-time executive at a Fortune 500 company. Has $1.8M in company stock (cost basis: $200,000, unrealized gain: $1.6M). Total portfolio: $2.4M. Retiring in 2 years.

The problem: 75% of portfolio in one stock is extreme concentration risk. However, selling all at once would trigger $320,000+ in federal capital gains taxes plus state taxes. Strategies: (1) Systematic diversification: Sell $200,000–$300,000 in stock annually to stay within the 15% long-term capital gains bracket. Each year she fills up the 0%/15% LTCG brackets before retirement. (2) Charitable giving: Donate appreciated shares to a donor-advised fund (DAF) — receives full fair market value deduction, no capital gains tax on donated shares. (3) Exchange fund: Contribute stock to a partnership; after 7 years, receive a diversified basket of stocks without immediate gain recognition (complex, accredited investor only). (4) Protective options: Buy puts or collars to limit downside while retaining upside.

title: Case Study: First-Generation College Student Saving for Education
text:
Profile: Rosa, 16, first-generation college aspirant. Parents earn $55,000/year combined. Older sister will start college next year. Goal: minimize student debt.

Key strategies for Rosa's family: (1) 529 plan: Still valuable even with 2 years remaining — contributions grow tax-free and state tax deduction may apply. $5,000 invested at 6% for 2 years grows to $5,600. (2) FAFSA: File as early as possible each year (opens October 1). At $55,000 income, Rosa likely qualifies for significant need-based aid. (3) Community college transfer path: 2 years community college + 2 years state university dramatically reduces total cost. (4) Merit scholarships: Strong GPA and extracurriculars open merit aid at many schools. (5) Work-study: Federal work-study programs provide part-time income. Average student debt should target no more than first-year expected salary — critical rule to prevent debt crisis.

title: Case Study: Late Start to Retirement Savings at 50
text:
Profile: Steven, 50, just becoming serious about retirement after spending his 30s-40s paying off debt and raising four children. Current savings: $45,000 in 401(k). Income: $88,000. Goal: retire comfortably at 67.

Reality check and plan: Steven has 17 years — still meaningful time. Maximize strategies: (1) Catch-up contributions: At 50, he can contribute $30,500 to 401(k) in 2024. Maximizing this for 17 years at 7% return = approximately $1,000,000. Combined with existing $45,000, total reaches $1,100,000+. (2) Social Security: Earning $88,000 through age 67 builds substantial Social Security — projected $2,500–$3,000/month. (3) Delay retirement to 67: Working until full retirement age maximizes Social Security and gives portfolio more time to grow. (4) Housing equity: If home is paid off, downsizing at retirement can free $150,000–$200,000. (5) Part-time work in early retirement (67–72) reduces withdrawal rate and portfolio depletion risk significantly.

title: Case Study: Dual-Income Couple Optimizing Tax Brackets
text:
Profile: Chris (45) and Jamie (43), both working professionals. Combined income: $320,000. Effective federal tax rate: 24–32%. Looking to minimize tax burden and optimize retirement savings.

Tax optimization strategies: (1) Maximize both 401(k) pre-tax contributions ($46,000 total) — reduces taxable income by $46,000 and saves $12,000–$15,000 in federal taxes annually. (2) HSA contribution if HDHP eligible — $8,300 family contribution. (3) Backdoor Roth IRA for each ($7,000 each, $14,000 total) — income too high for direct Roth. (4) Review 529 plans for children — state tax deduction if available. (5) Tax-loss harvesting in taxable account. (6) Charitable bunching: Contribute 2–3 years of charitable donations to a donor-advised fund (DAF) in one year to exceed standard deduction and itemize, then give from DAF in following years. Annual tax savings from full optimization: potentially $20,000–$30,000/year.

title: Case Study: Inheriting a Home — Keep, Sell, or Rent?
text:
Profile: Nicole, 44, inherited parents' home (value: $450,000, no mortgage, parents' cost basis: $80,000). She owns her own home and does not need to live in the inherited property.

Tax analysis: Under current tax law, inherited property receives a 'stepped-up' basis to the fair market value at the date of death. Nicole's new cost basis is $450,000 — meaning she can sell immediately with zero capital gains tax. This is one of the most valuable but least understood provisions in tax law. Options: (1) Sell now: Receive $450,000 tax-free (after selling costs). Best if she prefers liquidity and doesn't want to be a landlord. (2) Rent: If market rent is $2,500/month ($30,000/year), gross yield is 6.7%. But landlord responsibilities, vacancy, and maintenance must be factored in. (3) Keep: Only if she intends to use it as a second home or future residence. Recommendation: Sell and diversify unless there is strong personal attachment or rental economics are compelling.

title: Case Study: Newlywed Financial Planning
text:
Profile: Alex and Sam, both 28, recently married. Alex: $75,000 income, $30,000 savings. Sam: $62,000 income, $18,000 savings. Combined student loans: $42,000 at 5.8%.

First steps for financial unity: (1) Open joint accounts for shared expenses while maintaining individual accounts for personal spending — 'yours, mine, and ours' structure works well. (2) Update beneficiary designations on all accounts and life insurance policies. (3) Review insurance: may be able to save on health insurance by joining one employer's plan. (4) Discuss financial goals and values — major source of marital stress is financial disagreement. (5) Debt strategy: Both income levels suggest moderate debt-to-income. Accelerate loan payoff by directing one income toward loans. (6) Home purchase timeline: If buying in 3–5 years, start a dedicated down payment savings account in HYSAs. (7) Emergency fund: Build to 3–6 months of combined expenses ($20,000–$25,000). Time is their greatest advantage — maximizing Roth IRA contributions now generates extraordinary tax-free wealth over 40 years.

title: Case Study: International Employee with U.S. and Foreign Assets
text:
Profile: Yuki, 39, Japanese national working in the U.S. on an H-1B visa. Has retirement savings in Japan (equivalent of $120,000) and has been building U.S. accounts for 8 years (401k: $95,000, taxable: $40,000). Plans to eventually return to Japan.

Key complexities: (1) FBAR/FATCA filing: U.S. tax residents with more than $10,000 in foreign accounts must file FinCEN Form 114 (FBAR). Foreign financial assets over $50,000 require Form 8938. Non-compliance penalties are severe. (2) Japanese pension (iDeCo/NISA equivalents): Consult a cross-border tax specialist on whether Japanese accounts are covered by the U.S.-Japan tax treaty. (3) 401(k) at departure: Options include leaving in plan, rolling to IRA, or distributing (with taxes/penalty). IRA is generally best to maintain tax deferral. (4) Social Security totalization: U.S.-Japan totalization agreement prevents double Social Security taxation and may allow combined benefit. Cross-border financial planning requires specialized expertise.

title: Case Study: Lottery Winner — Managing a Windfall
text:
Profile: Robert, 52, won a state lottery jackpot of $2M (after-tax, lump sum). He currently earns $55,000/year and has $40,000 in savings.

Critical first steps — the 'lottery curse' is real: (1) Tell no one except immediate family and advisors. (2) Assemble a team: CPA (tax professional), estate attorney, and fee-only fiduciary CFP. Do not make any major financial decisions for 6–12 months. (3) Park the money: Initially in Treasury bills or FDIC-insured accounts while developing a plan. (4) Tax planning: The $2M is already after-tax. Any investment income is taxable going forward. (5) Avoid common mistakes: Do not loan money to family/friends, do not buy a boat/vacation home immediately, do not quit job in the first year. (6) Investment plan: $2M at 4% withdrawal = $80,000/year, replacing and exceeding current income. Invest in low-cost diversified index portfolio. $2M can provide lifelong income if managed prudently.

title: Case Study: Philanthropic Planning with Donor-Advised Fund
text:
Profile: Henry and Grace, 65 and 62, both retired with $3.2M in investments and a strong desire to give back to their community. Annual charitable giving: $25,000. They hold $200,000 in appreciated stock with a cost basis of $40,000.

Charitable strategy optimization: (1) Contribute appreciated stock to a Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) at Fidelity Charitable or Schwab Charitable. They receive a charitable deduction of $200,000 (full fair market value), avoid $24,000+ in capital gains taxes, and the fund can grow and be distributed to charities over time. (2) Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD): Once they reach age 70½, they can direct up to $105,000/year from IRA to charity as a QCD — counts toward RMD, reduces taxable income. (3) Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT): For larger gifts, provides income to donors during lifetime, remainder to charity — charitable deduction now. (4) Legacy planning: Name DAF or specific charities as beneficiaries of IRA assets — highly tax-efficient since charities pay no tax on inherited IRAs.

title: Case Study: Renter Debating Whether to Buy a Home
text:
Profile: Daniel, 33, renting for $1,800/month in a city. He has saved $80,000 for a potential down payment. Comparable homes cost $450,000. Current mortgage rate: 6.75%.

Buy vs. rent analysis: Monthly cost of ownership: $2,250 mortgage (30yr at 6.75%) + $400 property tax + $150 insurance + $300 maintenance = $3,100/month total cost of ownership vs. $1,800 rent. The 'price-to-rent ratio' is 450,000 / (1,800 × 12) = 20.8 — suggesting renting may be more financially efficient currently (ratio over 20 generally favors renting). However: (1) Mortgage payments build equity over time. (2) Historical home price appreciation averages 3–4%/year. (3) Psychological value of ownership and stability. (4) If planning to stay 7+ years, buying becomes more favorable. Daniel's $80,000 provides only an 18% down payment. Consider waiting for rates to decline or prices to adjust. Investing the $80,000 in index funds may outperform home equity over 5–7 years.

title: Case Study: Family Business Succession Planning
text:
Profile: William, 68, owner of a family manufacturing business valued at $4M. He has three children — one works in the business, two do not. Estate: $6M total. He wants to retire at 72 and treat children equitably.

Succession options: (1) Sale to child who works in business: At $4M value, cash purchase is difficult. Seller-financed installment sale over 10–15 years provides retirement income. Valuation discount for gift/intra-family sale — GRAT or family limited partnership can reduce estate tax exposure. (2) ESOP (Employee Stock Ownership Plan): Tax-advantaged sale to employees — seller can defer capital gains via Section 1042 rollover if ESOP owns 30%+ after sale. Provides employee ownership culture. (3) Third-party sale: Maximize value, simplify estate distribution — each child inherits cash equally. (4) Estate tax planning: Current $13.6M exemption ($27.2M married). If TCJA expires in 2026, exemption drops to ~$6M — urgency to use remaining exemption before expiration. Consult estate attorney immediately.

title: Case Study: Non-Profit Employee Maximizing Benefits
text:
Profile: Jessica, 37, program director at a non-profit, earning $65,000. She has access to both a 403(b) and a 457(b) plan through her employer — a powerful but underutilized combination.

Contribution strategy: (1) The key advantage: 403(b) and 457(b) limits are independent. She can contribute $23,000 to the 403(b) AND $23,000 to the 457(b) = $46,000 total in 2024 at age 37. (2) Additional advantage of 457(b): No 10% early withdrawal penalty — funds can be accessed at separation from service at any age. This is powerful for those considering early retirement or career changes. (3) Priority: First capture any employer match on 403(b). Then consider after-tax implications. (4) Student loan note: If Jessica has remaining loans from an academic background, the non-profit sector qualifies for PSLF after 10 years of qualifying payments. (5) At $65K income, contributing $46K is aggressive but possible with disciplined budgeting — saves $10,000–$13,000 in federal taxes annually.
